projects.name: UK Tech Job Opportunities
projects.author: Ebenezer Obi
projects.description: This is an interactive Shiny web application that analyzes and visualizes the evolution of technology-related job opportunities across the United Kingdom from 2019 to 2023. By leveraging job posting data, this project offers insights into shifting demands in job roles, required technical skills, salary trends, and geographic hiring patterns.
projects.detailedDescription: Developed using R, Shiny, and various data visualization libraries, this project demonstrates expertise in data cleaning, statistical analysis, interactive dashboard design, and web-based deployment. It also reflects a strong understanding of real-world employment trends and the value of data-driven decision-making in career and business strategy.
problems: Shifts in demand for roles, skills, and salaries are not always visible without deep analysis.
Emerging tech trends have disrupted hiring patterns.
Job seekers and recruiters lack accessible tools to explore these trends interactively.
goals: Identify trends in job titles, required skills, salaries, and hiring regions.
Build an interactive, user-friendly dashboard using Shiny to visualize the data.
Enable users to filter by year, location, job title, and skill for personalized insights.

findings.text: The United Kingdom according to these dataset recorded a total of 364 Tech related jobs from 2019 to 2023.
Although a pandemic occured in 2020, there was total job listing of 44 and they were all Full-time, suggesting they were listed at the start of year or post lockdown.
In regards to experience level, there seem to be no much opportunities for current student seeking an internship or fresh graduates looking to break into the corporate world.

challenges: Deploying the whole program to shinyapps.io.
Github commit hangs in local file instead of pushing to the remote repository.
Slow dashboard loading on shinyapps.io.
solutions: Ran error logs and removed rsconnect declarations present in the script.
Clearly set all assets in directory before cloning afresh and pushing through terminal.
Optimized plots using plotly + limited default dataset size

projects.name: Lofis Restaurant App
projects.author: Ebenezer Obi
projects.description: This project is a UX/UI mobile app prototype designed for a modern restaurant, built using Figma. The goal was to create a seamless and inclusive experience for users to easily make reservations, order food, view dietary information, and track deliveries in real time.
projects.detailedDescription: The design focuses on accessibility, user diversity, and intuitive navigation. Key features include dietary filters (vegan, gluten-free, allergy-safe), a simplified booking process, and a live map for delivery tracking. Personas ranged from busy professionals to elderly users, ensuring the app meets a wide spectrum of user needs.
problems: Users find the reservation process too time-consuming and confusing.
Dietary restrictions are hard to filter and view in the menu.
Users experience uncertainty about food delivery status.
Older users struggle with navigating the app interface.
goals: Simplify the reservation flow with fewer steps and clear prompts.
Implement intuitive dietary filters and display clear ingredient/allergy info.
Integrate a real-time delivery tracker with live map and ETA updates.
Design an accessible, senior-friendly UI with larger touch targets and simple language.

solutions: Simplify the reservation process by providing a one-click reservation feature and a calendar view with availability.
Add a dietary filter that can display specific dietary needs.
Redesign the menu to be categorized by food types.
Implement a live map that shows the real-time location and time of the delivery.

projects.name: WebSecura
projects.author: Ebenezer Obi
projects.description: WebSecura is a web-based security analysis tool designed to help developers, security professionals, and everyday users detect and understand vulnerabilities in any public-facing website. It performs comprehensive scanning using OWASP Top 10 principles, HTTP security header checks, SSL/TLS validation, and more — all wrapped in a sleek, intuitive UI.
problems: Users lack easy-to-use tools to perform real-time website security checks.
Most vulnerability scanners are too technical or require installation/setup.
Security headers, SSL certs, and basic OWASP protections are often overlooked.
There's no quick feedback loop for developers to assess basic site security compliance.
goals: Offer one-click scanning for any public URL
Provide clear, human-readable recommendations for each failed check
Support real-time scanning with detailed status output
Make security insights accessible and understandable to everyone

keyFunctions.text: Instantly runs over 15 web security checks including SSL/TLS validation, HTTP headers, XSS, and more.
Provides clear pass/fail statuses with human-readable descriptions and remediation tips for each issue.
Clean and responsive design ensures a smooth experience across desktop and mobile devices.

challenges: Some sites failed due to certificate trust issues.
Parsing headers across inconsistent server responses was tricky.
Structuring 15+ checks into readable categories required thoughtful UI planning.
solutions: Solved this by isolating certificate verification errors and informing the user instead of blocking the scan.
Added robust fallback logic to normalize and interpret results correctly.
Used status tags and icons to make output more scannable.

projects.name: E-Commerce Dashboard
projects.author: Ebenezer Obi
projects.description: This project presents an end-to-end e-commerce analytics solution — from raw transactional data to an interactive, insight-driven dashboard. Using Python (Pandas, NumPy) for data cleaning and enrichment, and Google Looker Studio for visualization, the project showcases key business intelligence workflows for sales, customers, and product performance analysis.
projects.detailedDescription: The dashboard provides a unified view of: Sales KPIs (Revenue, Orders, AOV), Customer demographics and behaviour, Product performance across categories and brands.
problems: E-commerce data was scattered across multiple CSV files with inconsistent product and customer IDs.
No unified system existed to track overall performance or key business metrics.
Limited visibility into which products, customers, or categories drove the most revenue.
goals: Consolidate all raw datasets into one clean, analytics-ready model.
Build a dynamic dashboard to monitor sales, customers, and product insights.
Enable quick identification of top performers and growth opportunities.

findings.text: A huge part of the total revenue was generated from Electronic products, highlighting other category areas the business needs to focus on.
Male customers spent on average 411% in entire order more than other age groups, indicating a key target demographic.
The entire dataset spanned through May to June and the revenue recorded can be possibly linked to mid-year promotions or seasonal shifts.

challenges: Product IDs in the raw basket data were inconsistent (numeric codes not matching product details).
Missing links between datasets (no direct foreign keys between orders and customers).
Limited time range (only two months of data) made long-term trend analysis difficult.
solutions: Used Python to regenerate a clean, standardized product_id system (e.g., P001–P200) and mapped basket entries to new product records.
Built relational joins in Python (Pandas merge) to create a unified dataset.
Shifted focus to daily-level performance and cross-category insights to maximize business relevance.

projects.name: Student Performance Predictor
projects.author: Ebenezer Obi
projects.description: A production-ready machine learning web application that predicts student performance levels using a Gaussian Naive Bayes classifier built entirely from scratch. The interactive dashboard provides real-time predictions with confidence analysis, feature importance visualization, and comprehensive model evaluation metrics — transforming academic coursework into a deployable product.
projects.detailedDescription: The application predicts student performance (High/Medium/Low) based on five behavioral features: part-time job status, absence days, extracurricular activities, weekly study hours, and gender. Built without sklearn, the classifier implements Bayes' Theorem with Gaussian probability distributions, log-space calculations for numerical stability, and custom feature contribution analysis. The dark-themed UI provides confidence thresholds, uncertainty warnings, probability distributions, and interactive Plotly visualizations.
problems: Machine learning models are often black boxes — users receive predictions without understanding confidence levels or feature contributions.
Academic ML projects rarely make it to production, remaining as Jupyter notebooks or command-line scripts.
Naive Bayes implementations in sklearn hide the underlying mathematics, limiting deep understanding of probabilistic classification.
Low-confidence predictions (e.g., 50.7%) are often presented with the same certainty as high-confidence ones, misleading decision-makers.
goals: Implement Gaussian Naive Bayes from scratch to demonstrate understanding of Bayes' Theorem, Gaussian probability density functions, and log-probability calculations.
Build a production-ready web interface that makes ML predictions accessible to non-technical users.
Create a confidence threshold system that honestly communicates prediction uncertainty.
Visualize feature contributions to make the classification process interpretable and educational.

findings.text: The model achieves 56% test accuracy on behavioral features alone, demonstrating that academic performance depends on many factors beyond student behavior — a realistic and honest assessment.
Extreme class imbalance (0.1% Low performers) reveals dataset limitations. The model struggles with rare classes, highlighting the importance of balanced training data in real-world ML applications.

Confidence thresholds prevent misleading predictions: the system warns users when predictions are uncertain (margin <5% between top classes) rather than presenting low-confidence results as definitive.

challenges: Probability density values for categorical features with low variance exceeded 400,000, making raw likelihoods uninterpretable for users.
The dataset contained severe class imbalance (High: 57.7%, Medium: 42.2%, Low: 0.1%), causing prediction bias.
SVG icons initially rendered as raw code in Streamlit's markdown, breaking the UI design.
Plotly charts rejected 'weight' parameter in font configurations, causing runtime errors during visualization.
solutions: Developed a relative importance visualization that normalizes likelihood values across classes to percentages, making feature contributions intuitive without sacrificing mathematical accuracy.
Implemented a three-tier confidence system (High ≥70%, Moderate 55-69%, Low <55%) with margin detection to alert users when predictions are uncertain.
Created custom SVG icon helper functions that inject styles directly into the opening tag, ensuring proper inline rendering in Streamlit components.
Stripped invalid Plotly font parameters and configured all charts for dark theme with proper color variables, grid styling, and text contrast.

projects.name: Spotify Prediction
projects.author: Ebenezer Obi
projects.description: An end-to-end data product that predicts a user’s future Spotify Wrapped using historical streaming data.
projects.detailedDescription: The project ingests extended Spotify listening history, applies time-series forecasting to predict top artists, songs, albums, and total listening time for the upcoming year, and presents the results in a fully animated Spotify Wrapped–inspired web experience.
problems: Spotify Wrapped is retrospective and offers no forward-looking insights into user behavior.
Raw streaming data is noisy, high-volume, and difficult to translate into meaningful trends.
Analytical results are often disconnected from user-friendly product experiences.
goals: Predict future listening behavior (artists, songs, albums, minutes listened) using historical data.
Translate model outputs into a compelling, product-quality user experience.
Build a reproducible pipeline that connects data science outputs directly to a frontend application.

findings.text: Listening behavior shows strong artist-level persistence, making top artists more predictable than individual tracks.
Aggregating monthly listening data significantly improves forecast stability compared to daily data.
A small subset of artists accounts for a disproportionate share of future listening time, reflecting long-term user affinity.

challenges: High variance and sparsity in track-level listening data.
Negative or unrealistic predictions from time-series models.
Bridging the gap between analytical outputs and an animated, consumer-grade UI.
solutions: Focused modeling on top artists and tracks to reduce noise and improve signal quality.
Applied post-processing constraints (e.g., clipping negative forecasts) to ensure realistic predictions.
Exported model results as structured JSON to enable seamless integration with a React + animation-driven frontend.

projects.name: Clouddey
projects.author: Ebenezer Obi
projects.description: Clouddey is a cloud-based temporary file-sharing application that allows users to securely upload files, set passwords and expiration times, and share download links via email. Files automatically expire and self-delete, ensuring privacy and minimal cloud storage usage.
problems: Files shared via messaging apps remain permanently accessible.
Sensitive files are often shared without protection.
Sending files manually is inefficient.
goals: Build a system where files automatically expire and self-delete.
Implement optional password protection with limited access attempts.
Automate secure link generation and email delivery to recipients.

keyFunctions.text: Serverless architecture simplifies scaling, vercel functions handled backend logic without managing servers.
Security must be enforced on the backend, password validation and attempt limiting cannot rely on frontend logic.
Ephemeral storage requires careful lifecycle control, auto-deletion logic must handle both expiration and download-triggered deletion reliably.

challenges: Handling file uploads in a serverless environment.
Preventing brute-force password attempts.
Managing expiration timers reliably.
solutions: Used Formidable v3 and temporary /tmp storage before moving files to Vercel Blob.
Implemented server-side attempt limiting (2 attempts max).
Stored timestamps and validated expiration during every download request, triggering automatic deletion when expired.

projects.name: Lofis Restaurant Website
projects.author: Ebenezer Obi
projects.description: Full-stack restaurant management system with customer ordering portal and admin dashboard. Features include menu management, order processing, user authentication, role-based access, and real-time analytics. Built with ASP.NET Core, EF Core, SQL Server, and deployed to production.
problems: Manual restaurant order management is time-consuming and error-prone.
Customer ordering systems often lack real-time menu updates and order tracking.
Restaurant data (sales, inventory, customer activity) is difficult to analyze without dedicated tools.
goals: Build a dual-interface system: customer-facing storefront and comprehensive admin dashboard.
Implement real-time order management with status tracking and role-based access control.
Provide actionable analytics with interactive visualizations for business insights.

keyFunctions.text: Role-based authentication separates customer and admin experiences, ASP.NET Core Identity handles secure registration, login, and authorization middleware.
Entity Framework Core manages complex relationships (users, orders, basket items, food items) with foreign keys, ensuring data integrity across transactions.
RESTful API controllers feed Chart.js visualizations, aggregating real-time metrics like revenue trends, order status distribution, and top-selling items without page reloads.

challenges: Deploying to a subfolder (/Lofis) on Plesk broke all routing and API calls.
Cart data persistence across sessions required careful state management.
Identity scaffolding conflicts caused duplicate registration errors.
solutions: Configured UsePathBase middleware and updated all navigation/API calls to use Razor's @Url.Content helper for path-base-aware URLs.
Created BasePageModel pattern with LoadCartCountAsync method, ensuring cart count displays consistently across all pages via database queries.
Consolidated Identity registration by removing AddDefaultIdentity and using AddIdentity with AddDefaultUI to prevent service duplication.
